hero-title: Why learn about a thing by playing with it?
Why learn it by playing with it yourself?
Why learn it by playing with it?
hero-lede: AI gives you answers that sound smooth and sure, but it never shows how it got there. So the first real skill is being able to tell real understanding apart from a good-sounding guess — both in yourself and in the machine. Everything here is built to help that skill grow, not to hand you the answers. Here is the argument, built up from the start — and you are free to disagree with any part of it.

AI gives you fluent, confident answers, but it never shows its working — so the first real skill is telling genuine understanding from a plausible guess, in yourself and in the machine. Everything here is built to grow that skill, not to hand you conclusions. Here's the argument, from the ground up — and you're free to disagree with any of it.

AI hands you fluent, confident answers with none of the working shown — so the first real skill is telling a genuine understanding from a plausible guess, in yourself and in the machine. Everything here is built to grow that skill, not to hand you conclusions. Here's the argument, from the ground up — and you're free to disagree with any of it.

AI hands you fluent, confident answers with no working shown — so the first skill is telling genuine understanding from a plausible guess, in yourself and in the machine. This grows that skill rather than handing you conclusions. The argument, from the ground up — disagree freely.

Fluent answers, no working shown — so the first skill is telling real understanding from a plausible guess, in you and in the machine. Built to grow that, not hand you conclusions. Yours to disagree with.
augment: The phrase augmented intelligence comes from Douglas Engelbart. Back in 1962 he argued that tools should lift what a person can do, not take the person's place. That is the whole idea here. If a page just told you what to think about AI, it would be doing the very opposite of what it stands for — making your conclusions for you. So the aim is to strengthen your own judgment, and to leave the deciding to you.

The phrase augmented intelligence comes from Douglas Engelbart, who argued back in 1962 that tools should raise what a person can do, not stand in for the person. That's the whole frame here. If a page simply told you what to think about AI, it would be doing the opposite of what it preaches — automating your conclusions. So the aim is to strengthen your judgment and leave the deciding to you.

The phrase augmented intelligence comes from Douglas Engelbart, who argued back in 1962 that tools should raise what a human can do, not stand in for the human. That's the whole frame here. If a page simply told you what to think about AI, it would be doing the opposite of what it preaches — automating your conclusions. So the aim is to amplify your judgment and leave the deciding to you.

Augmented intelligence is Douglas Engelbart's phrase: in 1962 he argued tools should raise what a human can do, not replace the human. That's the frame. A page that told you what to think about AI would automate your conclusions — the opposite of its own claim. So: amplify your judgment, leave the deciding to you.

Engelbart's phrase, augmented intelligence (1962): tools should raise what a human can do, not replace them. A page that told you what to think would automate your conclusions — the opposite. So it amplifies your judgment, and leaves the deciding to you.
manip: Prose can describe a moving system, but it cannot move. Bret Victor spent a whole career on a different way: show the data, give feedback the moment you act, and let people work the system with their own hands. Michael Nielsen's Reinventing Explanation makes the same case — that interactive, visual media can explain things plain words simply cannot reach. You tend to keep what you touched, more than what you were told.

You tend to keep what you touched, not what you were told.

Prose describes a moving system but can't move. Bret Victor's alternative: show the data, feedback the instant you act, hands on the system. Michael Nielsen's Reinventing Explanation argues the same — interactive media reach what prose can't. You keep what you touched, not what you were told.
visible: The cognitive scientist Judy Fan calls these things cognitive tools — number lines, diagrams, graphs, even augmented-reality overlays — things people have invented to make the invisible visible, so each generation can build on the one before. Her bigger point is a loop: we find useful abstractions about the world, then build new things with them — and the building makes something new visible to find. That loop is the shape of this whole site: discover → explore → build → and round again.

craft: This isn't just a hunch. A whole line of people turned the interactive explanation into a real craft — Nicky Case, Amit Patel's Red Blob Games, Dan Shiffman, Jack Schaedler, Vi Hart, William Ngan — and Case even wrote down the patterns (Puzzle It Out, Place Your Bets, Role Play, Sandbox Mode). It is only honest to add that the field is small and its makers are human: Case has written openly about burnout, and some collections haven't grown in years. We are standing on what they built, not pretending we invented it.

vary: David Deutsch gives us the test. A real explanation is hard to vary — change one part and the whole thing breaks. A weak one is easy to vary: it could just as smoothly have "explained" the opposite. When AI hands you an explanation, ask yourself whether you could take out or swap a piece and still land on the same conclusion. If you could, don't trust it yet. That test is yours to use — here, and everywhere else.

David Deutsch gives the test. A genuine explanation is hard to vary — change one part and it breaks. A weak one is easy to vary: it would have "explained" the opposite just as smoothly. When AI hands you an explanation, ask whether you could remove or swap a piece and still reach the same conclusion. If you could, don't trust it yet. That test is yours to apply — here, and everywhere else.

David Deutsch gives the test. A genuine explanation is hard to vary — change one part and it breaks. A weak one is easy to vary: it would have "explained" the opposite just as smoothly. Given an AI explanation, ask whether you could remove or swap a piece and still reach the same conclusion. If so, don't trust it yet. The test is yours to apply — here and everywhere.

Deutsch's test: a genuine explanation is hard to vary — change a part and it breaks. A weak one is easy to vary; it'd have "explained" the opposite as smoothly. Could you swap a piece of an AI's explanation and still reach the same conclusion? If so, don't trust it yet. The test is yours to apply.
stick: Understanding that has faded away by next week isn't much use. Bringing something back to mind strengthens it far more than reading it again (the testing effect); and spacing that recall so it lands just before you'd forget resets the forgetting curve. That's why small recall prompts are woven through these pages — and why there's a page that turns your own recall data into a way of seeing how memory works, both yours and the machine's.

memory-cta: The cards you just answered turned into data. This page draws that data — your forgetting curve and your ladder — and places it next to the way an LLM "remembers", so you can see where the two are alike and where they part ways.

found-note: The argument above is why we teach in this way. These short reads are the what — AI starting from nothing, in plain words. There is no order you have to follow.
